title: hack/release.sh: Add a release script
body:
@mheon had expressed interest in a lovely release script on IRC ;).  Here's my attempt to encode the changes from the v0.5.4 release branch.  I've also added tag signing, so you may be prompted for your passphrase during that step.

The spec sed also bumps the Python package version to match.  I dunno if that's the intended behavior or not, but it may not matter with the Python code destined for a separate repo soon.

comments.body:
> The spec `sed` also bumps the Python package version to match. I dunno if that's the intended behavior or not...

I think that is the intended behavior based on @baude's comment [here][1].

[1]: https://github.com/projectatomic/libpod/issues/786#issuecomment-390682012

I've pushed 96c1535 -> 12bd368a to bump the Python package in lockstep.  @jwhonce, anything to bump besides `setup.py`?  Do you have a preference between `${NEXT_VERSION}` and `${NEXT_VERSION}-dev` in the post-release bump (e.g. the post v0.5.4 70672652)?

I'm not sure why that commit went with `0.6.1-dev` instead of `0.6.0` or `0.5.5`.  Is the pattern `0.$(month).$(count)` with `$(count)` starting at 1?  If so, are we pretty sure we're hitting 1.0 in 2018?  I guess we could use 0.13.1 for the first 2019-01 release if we miss...
@wking Correct, we decided on 0.Month.Week for pre-1.0 versioning. If we need to keep going after we hit 0.12.x, we'll probably go up to 0.13.x as you predicted.

I've pushed 12bd368a -> 8bac6855 to make the `0.${month}.${count}` versioning automatic.  I didn't use `${week}` for the patch-level entry, partly because [Coreutils' date doesn't support][1] a "week of the month" formatter, and partly because we may at some point want to cut a hotfix release midweek.  I'm on the fence about whether the additional lines of shell are worth the trouble, or whether we want the caller to specify the current and next release on the command line.  @mheon, do you have a preference?

[1]: https://www.gnu.org/software/coreutils/manual/html_node/Date-conversion-specifiers.html#Date-conversion-specifiers
@wking I think current and next being manually specified should be fine

> I think current and next being manually specified should be fine

Ok, I've gone back to that with 8bac6855 -> 8405ff9b.  8405ff9b has the same diff as the earlier 12bd368, but the commit message has been updated to explain both the current versioning scheme and why we are not automating it.
This LGTM. Thanks for the work @wking

:pushpin: Commit 8405ff9 has been approved by `rhatdan`

<!-- @rh-atomic-bot r=rhatdan 8405ff9baa2c0ce412d5a1ff6cad7e327ade0439 -->
Homu appears well and truly broken. Going to start manually merging things with green tests.
